channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Useful quantum computers could be built with as few as 10,000 qubits, team finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a dramatic shift in the quantum computing timeline, potentially accelerating practical quantum advantage by years or even decades. The reduction from millions to tens of thousands of qubits needed for fault-tolerant quantum computing brings these machines within reach of current scaling trajectories, with companies like IBM and Google already building systems with hundreds to thousands of qubits. The Caltech-Oratomic collaboration's error reduction approach could be the key breakthrough that transforms quantum computing from a laboratory curiosity into a commercially viable technology by 2030. This finding essentially moves the goalposts closer just as the field is gaining momentum, making the quantum computing revolution feel tangibly within our grasp.

Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today's rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require millions of qubits to work properly (qubits being the quantum equivalent to 1's and 0's in classical computers), the new results indicate that a fully realized quantum computer could be built with as few as 10,000 to 20,000 qubits. The need for fewer qubits means that quantum computers could, in theory, be operational by the end of the decade.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Learning and Generating Mixed States Prepared by Shallow Channel Circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's fundamental challenges: how to efficiently learn and recreate unknown quantum states from limited measurement data, which is crucial for everything from quantum sensing to state verification. The researchers prove that mixed quantum states prepared by shallow circuits can be learned and reproduced using only polynomial resources, even without knowing the original preparation method—a significant theoretical advance that could make quantum state learning practically feasible. What makes this particularly exciting is the connection to quantum generative models, suggesting these techniques could enable new approaches for generating complex quantum states efficiently, while also inspiring improvements to classical machine learning methods like diffusion models. The focus on "trivial phase" states provides a concrete framework for understanding which quantum states can be efficiently learned, offering both theoretical insights and potential practical algorithms for near-term quantum devices.

Learning quantum states from measurement data is a central problem in quantum information and computational complexity. In this work, we study the problem of learning to generate mixed states on a finite-dimensional lattice. Motivated by recent developments in mixed state phases of matter, we focus on arbitrary states in the trivial phase. A state belongs to the trivial phase if there exists a shallow preparation channel circuit under which local reversibility is preserved throughout the preparation. We prove that any mixed state in this class can be efficiently learned from measurement access alone. Specifically, given copies of an unknown trivial phase mixed state, our algorithm outputs a shallow local channel circuit that approximately generates this state in trace distance. The sample complexity and runtime are polynomial (or quasi-polynomial) in the number of qubits, assuming constant (or polylogarithmic) circuit depth and gate locality. Importantly, the learner is not given the original preparation circuit and relies only on its existence. Our results provide a structural foundation for quantum generative models based on shallow channel circuits. In the classical limit, our framework also inspires an efficient algorithm for classical diffusion models using only a polynomial overhead of training and generation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: High Performance Quantum Emulation for Chemistry Applications with Hyperion
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical bottleneck in quantum computing development: the gap between theoretical algorithms and available quantum hardware for chemistry applications. Hyperion's innovative SV-MPS hybrid approach cleverly sidesteps the traditional memory limitations that plague classical simulations of quantum chemistry problems, enabling researchers to test quantum algorithms on systems with up to 40 qubits while maintaining near-exact accuracy. The emulator's ability to handle realistic chemical systems at Full Configuration Interaction-level precision provides quantum algorithm developers with an essential testing ground for validating chemistry protocols before expensive quantum hardware becomes widely available. This type of high-performance classical emulation infrastructure is becoming increasingly valuable as the quantum computing field transitions from proof-of-concept demonstrations to practical applications in drug discovery and materials science.

The strategic demand for quantum hardware currently outpaces the availability of near-term devices, necessitating high-performance software emulators to validate novel protocols. We introduce Hyperion, a massively parallel, GPU-accelerated quantum emulator architected to bypass the classical memory walls inherent in strongly correlated quantum chemistry simulations. Hyperion leverages custom-optimized Sparse Matrix-Sparse Vector (SpMspV) kernels to natively accelerate exact matrix-vector multiplications, enabling strictly accurate State-Vector (SV) ADAPT-VQE simulations for up to 32 qubits on multi-node platforms. To scale beyond this hardware limit, we address the trade-off in pure Matrix Product State (MPS) emulators, where standard compression yields severe truncation errors and strict compression triggers intractable tensor rank explosions. We propose a novel partitioned emulation, namely the SV-MPS strategy: by routing non-interacting terms into an exact sparse SV core and delegating interacting terms to the MPS engine, this approach achieves emulation of 36 to 40 qubits with controlled approximations. This partitioning significantly reduces GPU resource requirements while maintaining robust accuracy across ADAPT-VQE iterations. Ultimately, Hyperion offers a high-fidelity platform dedicated to the development of new quantum algorithms for chemistry, enabling the modeling of realistic chemical systems at accuracies approaching the exact Full Configuration Interaction (FCI) / Complete Basis Set (CBS) limit.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Simultaneous operation of an 18-qubit modular array in germanium
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a significant milestone in semiconductor quantum computing, demonstrating that spin qubits in germanium can be scaled to 18 qubits while maintaining exceptional gate fidelities above 99.8%. The modular 2xN architecture is particularly promising because it provides a clear pathway for further scaling, addressing one of the most critical challenges facing semiconductor quantum processors. What makes this especially compelling is that germanium-based systems are naturally compatible with existing silicon chip manufacturing, potentially enabling the hybrid quantum-classical architectures needed for practical quantum computing. The simultaneous control and readout across all 18 qubits, combined with successful three-qubit entangled state generation, shows that these systems are moving beyond proof-of-concept toward genuinely scalable quantum processors.

Utility-scale quantum computing requires the integration and operation of a large-scale qubit register. Semiconductor spin qubits are a primary candidate for this, due to the prospects of building integrated hybrid quantum-classical architectures. However, scaling spin-qubit systems while preserving performance and control has remained a challenge. Here, we demonstrate the operation of an 18-qubit array in germanium based on an extendable 2xN architecture. We achieve simultaneous initialization, control, and readout across the entire array, enabled by parallel operation of modular unit cells. Across the array, we achieve average and median single-qubit gate fidelities of 99.8% and 99.9%, respectively. Finally, we characterize the nearest-neighbor exchange couplings throughout the device and implement high-quality controlled-Z gates to generate a three-qubit Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) state. These results demonstrate that spin-qubit arrays can be scaled while maintaining high-fidelity operation and establish a modular, extendable architecture for planar semiconductor quantum processors.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Tsim: Fast Universal Simulator for Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This open-source simulator addresses a critical bottleneck in quantum error correction research by dramatically accelerating the simulation of noisy quantum circuits through clever ZX diagram representations and GPU acceleration. The key breakthrough is achieving linear scaling with Clifford gates while only paying exponential costs for non-Clifford operations, making it possible to efficiently simulate the low-magic circuits typical in quantum error correction protocols. By maintaining compatibility with the widely-used Stim framework while adding support for T gates and arbitrary rotations, Tsim fills an important gap in the quantum computing simulation ecosystem and could significantly speed up the development of fault-tolerant quantum algorithms.

We present Tsim, an open-source high-throughput simulator for universal noisy quantum circuits targeting quantum error correction. Tsim represents quantum circuits as ZX diagrams, where Pauli channels are modeled as parameterized vertices. Diagrams are simplified via parameterized ZX rules, and then compiled for vectorized sampling with GPU acceleration. After the one-time compilation, one can sample detector or measurement shots in linear time in the number of Clifford gates and exponentially only in the number of non-Clifford gates. Tsim implements the Stim API and fully supports the Stim circuit format, extending it with T and arbitrary single-qubit rotation instructions. For low-magic circuits, Tsim throughput can match the sampling performance of Stim.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distilling Unitary Operations: A No-Go Theorem and Minimal Realization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: how to extract clean, useful quantum operations from inherently noisy physical gates. The authors prove a striking impossibility result - that you cannot universally purify arbitrary single-qubit gates using just two noisy copies - but then show that three copies provide the minimal architecture needed to beat trivial error mitigation strategies. This work establishes rigorous theoretical limits on "unitary purification" techniques, which could inform the design of more robust quantum algorithms and error correction schemes. The findings are particularly relevant as the field pushes toward fault-tolerant quantum computing, where understanding the fundamental boundaries of what's possible with noisy operations becomes crucial for practical quantum advantage.

Quantum gates executed on physical hardware are inevitably degraded by environmental noise. While state purification effectively distills static quantum resources, the dynamic execution of quantum algorithms requires a higher-order approach to mitigate errors on the operations themselves. In this work, we investigate unitary purification: the task of utilizing a quantum higher-order operation to partially restore the ideal action of an unknown unitary corrupted by a known noise model. Focusing on canonical depolarizing noise, we first reveal a fundamental operational obstruction. We prove that within the indefinite causal order framework, no nontrivial 2-slot higher-order operation can universally purify the set of single-qubit unitaries. Overcoming this strict limitation, we establish that a 3-slot architecture provides the minimal realization for non-trivial universal purification. We analytically derive the optimal average fidelity for the 3-slot regime, demonstrating that it strictly surpasses trivial strategies by systematically utilizing ancillary qubits as a quantum memory to absorb errors. Furthermore, we provide a concrete quantum circuit construction for this optimal higher-order operation. Our results establish the strict theoretical boundaries of distilling clean operations from noisy gates, offering immediate architectural insights for robust gate design.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: EPB joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Quantum Development
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals growing regional coordination in quantum development, with EPB's participation as a telecommunications provider highlighting the crucial role of infrastructure companies in building quantum networks. The Southeastern Quantum Collaborative represents a strategic approach to quantum advancement, pooling resources across universities and industry partners in a region that's been working to establish itself as a quantum hub alongside established centers like Silicon Valley and the Northeast corridor. EPB's involvement is particularly noteworthy given their experience with advanced fiber infrastructure, which will be essential for quantum communication networks and connecting quantum computers across distances. This type of regional collaboration could accelerate practical quantum deployments by creating concentrated expertise and shared resources in specific geographic areas.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — EPB has joined the Southeastern Quantum Collaborative (SQC) as an inaugural member. SQC is an association of universities, technology companies and research institutions working together to accelerate the advancement and real-world application of quantum technologies across the Southeast. Led by The University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), the collaborative brings together organizations from […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Properties of multiqubit variational quantum states representing weighted graphs and their computing with quantum programming
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research establishes a fascinating bridge between classical graph theory and quantum computing by showing how the structure of weighted graphs can be encoded in quantum states using simple variational circuits. The key insight that quantum properties like entanglement directly correlate with graph features such as vertex degrees opens up new possibilities for using quantum computers to analyze complex network problems. While the work is currently demonstrated on simulators with a star graph example, it represents an important step toward quantum algorithms that could potentially offer advantages for graph analysis tasks in fields ranging from social networks to molecular chemistry. The approach using basic RX and RZZ gates makes it particularly relevant for near-term quantum devices, where such shallow circuits are more feasible than deeper quantum algorithms.

We study multiqubit variational quantum states that can be considered as weighted quantum graph states. These states are constructed as single-layer variational circuits with $RX$ rotations and $RZZ$ entangling gates, corresponding to graphs of arbitrary structure. In general case of quantum graph states of arbitrary structure we derive the geometric measure of entanglement and evaluate quantum correlators. It is shown that these quantities are directly related to the degrees of the corresponding vertices in graph. As an example, we analyze the state associated with the star graph $K_{1,4}$ using noisy quantum computing on the AerSimulator. The results are in good agreement with theoretical predictions. These findings demonstrate a connection between graph structure and quantum properties, enabling the study of classical graphs via quantum computing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Two-Qubit Implementation of QAOA for MAX-CUT on an NV-Center Quantum Processor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents an important milestone in demonstrating QAOA on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers, a promising quantum platform that operates at room temperature without the need for dilution refrigerators. While the two-qubit MAX-CUT implementation is necessarily simple, it validates that NV centers can execute the core QAOA operations needed for combinatorial optimization problems, which could make quantum optimization more accessible in practical settings. The researchers had to develop clever workarounds for the NV center's non-projective optical readout, reconstructing computational basis states from fluorescence measurements to map the optimization landscape. This proof-of-principle work establishes NV centers as a viable alternative to superconducting and trapped-ion systems for near-term quantum algorithms, though significant scaling challenges remain before tackling industrially relevant optimization problems.

We report a proof-of-principle implementation of the quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) for the smallest nontrivial MAX-CUT instance on an NV-center-based quantum processor operating at room temperature. The two-qubit register is encoded in the electron spin and the ${}^{14}\mathrm{N}$ nuclear spin of a single NV$^-$ center. Using a minimization formulation of MAX-CUT, we implement a single-layer QAOA ansatz with native entangling and single-qubit control operations. Because the optical readout of the NV$^-$ center is not projective in the computational basis, we reconstruct computational-basis populations from averaged fluorescence signals and use them to determine the experimental QAOA cost landscape by scanning the variational parameters. These results show that the core elements of QAOA can be realized on this platform and establish a baseline for future improvements in phase tracking, coherence-preserving control, and scaling to larger problem sizes.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Multi-Mode Quantum Annealing for Variational Autoencoders with General Boltzmann Priors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article showcases a fascinating convergence of quantum annealing hardware and machine learning, demonstrating how D-Wave's 2000-qubit Advantage2 processor can enhance variational autoencoders by enabling more sophisticated energy-based priors that capture complex relationships in data. The breakthrough lies in using three different quantum annealing modes within a single system - diabatic annealing for unbiased training samples, standard annealing for generation, and conditional annealing with bias fields for targeted generation - effectively turning the quantum processor into a versatile sampling engine for machine learning. What makes this particularly significant is that it moves beyond the typical Gaussian assumptions that limit most VAEs, allowing the model to learn and sample from much richer latent representations while demonstrating practical advantages like faster convergence and better reconstruction quality. This work represents a compelling example of near-term quantum advantage in machine learning, showing how current quantum annealers can immediately enhance AI capabilities rather than waiting for fault-tolerant quantum computers.

Variational autoencoders (VAEs) learn compact latent representations of complex data, but their generative capacity is fundamentally constrained by the choice of prior distribution over the latent space. Energy-based priors offer a principled way to move beyond factorized assumptions and capture structured interactions among latent variables, yet training such priors at scale requires accurate and efficient sampling from intractable distributions. Here we present Boltzmann-machine--prior VAEs (BM-VAEs) trained using quantum annealing--based sampling in three distinct operational modes within a single generative system. During training, diabatic quantum annealing (DQA) provides unbiased Boltzmann samples for gradient estimation of the energy-based prior; for unconditional generation, slower quantum annealing (QA) concentrates samples near low-energy minima; for conditional generation, bias fields are added to direct sampling toward attribute-specific regions of the energy landscape (c-QA). Using up to 2000 qubits on a D-Wave Advantage2 processor, we demonstrate stable and efficient training across multiple datasets, with faster convergence and lower reconstruction loss than a Gaussian-prior VAE. The learned Boltzmann prior enables unconditional generation by sampling directly from the energy-based latent distribution, a capability that plain autoencoders lack, and conditional generation through latent biasing that leverages the learned pairwise interactions.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Simulation of Cranked Zirconium Isotopes: A Fixed-N Approach with a Structured Number-Conserving Ansatz
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most promising applications by demonstrating how variational quantum algorithms can simulate complex nuclear physics phenomena, specifically the rotational behavior of zirconium isotopes under different conditions. The researchers cleverly address a fundamental challenge in quantum simulation by developing a number-conserving ansatz that maintains physical constraints while keeping the quantum circuit tractable with just 42 parameters for their 8-qubit system. What makes this particularly significant is their introduction of a novel "pairing-coherence diagnostic" that sidesteps traditional measurement issues in exact number-conserving systems, potentially opening new pathways for studying nuclear structure on near-term quantum devices. While the results reflect a truncated model rather than fully converged spectroscopy, this work represents meaningful progress toward quantum advantage in computational nuclear physics, a field where classical methods often struggle with exponential scaling challenges.

We present a methodological study of quantum simulation of cranking in a Nilsson $+$ pairing Hamiltonian on a fixed deformation grid. The many-body Routhian is mapped to qubits via the Jordan--Wigner transformation and minimized using the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) in a truncated active space $(M)$. We employ a structured, number-conserving singles-and-doubles ansatz: double excitations implement pair transfer, while singles are restricted to the nonzero Coriolis-coupling graph of the active Nilsson basis. For $M=8$, this yields 42 parameters while preserving particle number exactly. Exact number conservation enforces $\langle P_k \rangle = 0$, so the conventional pairing gap $Δ_κ\propto G\left|\sum_k \langle P_k \rangle\right|$ vanishes identically. We instead introduce a fixed-$N$ pairing-coherence diagnostic, \[ Δ_{\mathrm{coh}} = G \sqrt{\sum_{k \neq l} \left| \langle P_k^\dagger P_l \rangle \right|}, \] used as a scalar measure of off-diagonal pair coherence rather than a BCS gap. We study even-even $^{80,82,84}$Zr. $^{80}$Zr shows a stable oblate minimum at $δ^\ast \approx -0.25$; $^{82}$Zr exhibits the strongest rotational evolution; $^{84}$Zr retains a robust prolate minimum with the largest neutron pairing coherence. These results reflect the present truncated model rather than converged spectroscopy. A cranked BCS calculation on the same grid serves as a qualitative baseline. Comparisons between $M=6$ and $M=8$ show stable trends but visible shifts, so no active-space convergence is claimed. The structured fixed-$N$ ansatz thus captures consistent isotope trends and provides a practical framework to analyze pairing via $Δ_{\mathrm{coh}}$.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unitary Encoding of Thermal States via Thermofield Dynamics on Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough approach to one of quantum computing's most persistent challenges: simulating thermal quantum states that naturally occur at finite temperatures. The researchers have developed a NISQ-friendly algorithm using Thermofield Dynamics that can prepare and evolve thermal states with only linear circuit depth scaling and basic gate operations, making it practically implementable on current noisy quantum devices. This work is particularly significant because thermal effects are typically viewed as obstacles in quantum computing, but here they become the feature rather than the bug, opening new possibilities for studying quantum thermodynamics, phase transitions, and even thermal machine learning models. The successful benchmarking results, including precise agreement with theoretical predictions for magnetic systems, suggest this could become a valuable tool for near-term quantum simulations where thermal effects play crucial roles.

Quantum computing has attracted the attention of the scientific community in the past few decades. However, despite some relevant advantages, near-term quantum devices remain severely limited by thermal effects, which induce decoherence and restrict coherent control at finite temperature. In this regard, this work reports a gate-based quantum algorithm that prepares the finite-temperature vacuum of Thermofield Dynamics (TFD) and tracks its real-time evolution. The circuit depth scales linearly with system size and requires only single-qubit rotations and nearest-neighbor CNOT gates, making it NISQ-friendly. We benchmark the protocol on the PennyLane simulator: magnetization of a spin-$1/2$ particle in a magnetic field agrees with the exact result $M(β)=\tanh(βω/2)$ to machine precision, and the coherent precession acquires a temperature-dependent damping that quantitatively matches the analytical TFD prediction. Our work provides a ready-to-deploy toolbox for thermal quantum simulations and opens a route to study dissipative phase transitions, quantum thermodynamics and thermal machine-learning models on near-term devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Brooklyn-based Pizza Makers Declare Quantum Supremacy in Qulinary Breakthrough
item.description:
Curator's Take: This appears to be a humorous April Fools' style piece rather than legitimate quantum computing research, with playful references like "anXovi" (a play on arXiv) and "pizza scientists" declaring "quantum supremacy in qulinary breakthrough." While the quantum computing field does benefit from creative outreach and science communication that makes complex concepts more accessible, readers should note this is likely satirical content rather than actual scientific progress. The article does highlight how quantum terminology has entered popular culture, though serious developments in quantum computing continue to emerge from academic institutions and tech companies rather than Brooklyn pizzerias. For those seeking genuine quantum computing news, it's worth distinguishing between legitimate research publications and entertaining but fictional content.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Sure, quantum science can be complex and intense, but now it can also be delicious, according to pizza makers from Brooklyn-based Aleesia and Roberto’s Fine Italian Diner and Pizzeria. In a study published on the premier pre-print server for the pizza industry, anXovi, a team of pizza scientists reported they […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Tunable information insulation induced by constraint mismatch
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates a fascinating quantum phenomenon where mismatched constraints at the junction of two quantum chains create an impenetrable barrier to information flow, essentially allowing researchers to engineer "quantum firewalls" that can be turned on and off at will. The work is particularly exciting because it shows how to deliberately fragment quantum systems into isolated regions with different thermal properties, creating materials that could be simultaneously hot and cold in different spatial regions. The authors' proposal for implementing this in Rydberg atom arrays using optical tweezers makes this more than just theoretical curiosity - it represents a practical pathway toward building quantum devices with unprecedented control over information flow and thermalization. This level of programmable quantum isolation could prove invaluable for protecting sensitive quantum computations from environmental interference while enabling new types of quantum simulations of exotic materials.

We study a composite model of two $1D$ $PXP$ chains with dual constraints, forming a junction that acts as an infinite kinematic barrier to quantum information exchange. Moreover, the hard wall at the junction which acts as a perfect reflector, preventing any quantum information leakage between the two sides of the composite chain, can be made permeable by relaxing the constraint at the junction sites. Multiple frozen junctions shatter the Hilbert space into disjoint Krylov fragments, the number of which increases exponentially with the engineered defects. Furthermore, the energy level statistics in each symmetry-resolved sector are strictly Poissonian, demonstrating that the tensor sum of the disjoint Hamiltonians results in a pure superposition of the chaotic spectra of the sub- $PXP$ chains. We also find that a chirally protected zero-energy mode can exist which has local peaks at the physical edges and within the bulk near the junction sites. This state is protected from hybridization with bulk states induced by any chirality preserving disorder. Due to the tensor product structure of the eigenfunctions, the non-zero energy scar states also multiply in number. Finally, we introduce novel Fock states with spatially tunable thermal and athermal regions. This architecture can be readily realized in programmable Rydberg atom platforms using optical tweezers, addressing beams and facilitation techniques.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Decay of the survival probability of a local excitation in multi-qubit platforms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical work tackles a fundamental question in multi-qubit quantum systems: how quickly does a localized quantum state spread out and lose its original form, a process crucial for understanding decoherence in superconducting quantum processors. The researchers discovered that the decay behavior depends primarily on whether the system's energy states are extended throughout the platform rather than on the specific nature of qubit interactions, suggesting a universal mechanism underlying quantum equilibration. This finding provides valuable theoretical benchmarks for experimentalists working with superconducting quantum devices, helping them predict and potentially control how quantum information spreads through their multi-qubit systems. The work bridges fundamental quantum mechanics with practical concerns about maintaining quantum coherence in scalable quantum computing platforms.

We present a theoretical study of the survival probability of a state initially prepared in the one-particle sector of a multi-qubit system. The motivation for our work is the ongoing laboratory development of multi-qubit platforms based on superconducting circuits. Using elementary concepts of random matrix theory, we obtain analytic expressions for the survival probability in mathematical models of platforms which, albeit stylized, have been previously shown to provide relevant benchmarks for experimental data. In particular, we show that the decay properties are sensitive to the property of the Hamilton operator to have extended states. The survival probability does not appear instead to depend on whether the interaction between qubits is described by a Gaussian orthogonal ensemble (often interpreted as a model of ''chaotic'' dynamics) or is modeled by an analytically solvable chain. We interpret this phenomenon as a manifestation of a general mechanism for the emergence of equilibration in purely unitary dynamics. Finally, under the same hypothesis of an initial preparation with projection on a large fraction of the extended eigenstates of the Hamilton operator, we show how to extend the classical Kac-Mazur-Montroll estimate of the return time to the quantum survival probability.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum machine learning for the quantum lattice Boltzmann method: Trainability of variational quantum circuits for the nonlinear collision operator across multiple time steps
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a fascinating convergence of quantum machine learning and computational fluid dynamics, addressing one of the most challenging aspects of quantum simulation: handling nonlinear dynamics that don't naturally fit quantum mechanics' linear evolution. The quantum lattice Boltzmann method has emerged as a promising approach for simulating fluid flows on quantum computers, but the nonlinear collision terms have remained a significant bottleneck. By training variational quantum circuits to approximate these nonlinear operators, the researchers are essentially teaching quantum computers to "think nonlinearly" through machine learning, potentially unlocking quantum advantages for complex fluid simulations used in everything from weather prediction to aerodynamics. The dual-architecture approach, with models optimized for either continuous multi-step evolution or high-precision single-step operations, suggests this could be a practical pathway toward quantum computational fluid dynamics at scale.

This study investigates the application of quantum machine learning (QML) to approximate the nonlinear component of the collision operator within the quantum lattice Boltzmann method (QLBM). To achieve this, we train a variational quantum circuit (VQC) to construct an operator $U$. When applied to the post-linear-collision quantum state $\ket{Ψ_i}$, this operator yields a final state $\ket{Ψ_f} = U\ket{Ψ_i}$ that successfully replicates the nonlinear collision dynamics derived from the Bhatnagar-Gross-Krook (BGK) approximation. Within this framework, we present two distinct architectures: the R1 model and the R2 model. The R1 model is designed for quantum simulations that involve multiple time steps without intermediate measurements, focusing on accurately capturing nonlinear dynamics in continuous evolution. In contrast, the R2 model is tailored to achieve the high-precision reconstruction of the nonlinear operator for a single time step with an unitary operator.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Teaching Approach Could Open The Door to Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial but often overlooked bottleneck in quantum computing's development: the education gap that limits how many people can enter this rapidly expanding field. While quantum hardware continues advancing at breakneck speed, the shortage of quantum-literate researchers, engineers, and developers threatens to slow real-world applications of these powerful machines. New teaching approaches that make quantum mechanics more accessible could dramatically expand the talent pipeline, potentially accelerating everything from drug discovery algorithms to cryptographic breakthroughs. The quantum revolution won't just depend on building better qubits—it will require building a quantum-ready workforce at scale.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum computing, an emergent technology with the potential to solve otherwise impossible problems, grew out of quantum mechanics, the branch of physics that explores how matter behaves at the tiniest of scales. In contrast to what’s known as a classical computer, the kind you and I use every day, a quantum computer can track many possibilities at once, enabling it to perform complex calculations in minutes that would take classical computers thousands of years (if at all). Recent advances […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: No quantum advantage implies improved bounds and classical algorithms for the binary paint shop problem
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research delivers a fascinating twist in the quantum advantage debate by using the absence of quantum speedup to actually improve classical algorithms. The authors cleverly reasoned that since QAOA with logarithmic depth showed no quantum advantage on the binary paint shop problem, there must exist better classical algorithms than previously known - and they proved themselves right by developing the Mean-Field Approximate Optimization Algorithm that outperforms both existing classical methods and quantum approaches. This work exemplifies how quantum computing research can drive classical algorithm innovation, even when quantum methods don't win, and highlights the ongoing challenge of finding problems where near-term quantum devices truly excel. The binary paint shop problem itself, while seemingly niche, represents a class of sparse optimization problems that are common in real-world applications, making these algorithmic insights broadly valuable.

The binary paint shop problem (BPSP) is an APX-hard optimization problem in which, given n car models that occur twice in a sequence of length 2n, the goal is to find a colouring sequence such that the two occurrences of each model are painted differently, while minimizing the number of times the paint is swapped along the sequence. A recent classical heuristic, known as the recursive star greedy (RSG) algorithm, is conjectured to achieve an expected paint swap ratio of 0.361, thereby outperforming the QAOA with circuit depth p = 7. Since the performance of the QAOA with logarithmic circuit depth is instance independent, the average paint swap ratio is upper-bounded by the QAOA, which numerical evidence suggests is approximately between 0.265 and 0.282. To provide hardware-relevant comparisons, we additionally implement the BPSP on a D-Wave Quantum Annealer Advantage 2, obtaining a minimum paint swap ratio of 0.320. Given that the QAOA with logarithmic circuit depth does not exhibit quantum advantage for sparse optimization problems such as the BPSP, this implies the existence of a classical algorithm that surpasses both the RSG algorithm and logarithmic depth QAOA. We provide numerical evidence that the Mean-Field Approximate Optimization Algorithm (MF-AOA) is one such algorithm that beats all known classical heuristics and quantum algorithms to date with a paint swap ratio of approximately 0.2799.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum algorithms for the fractional Poisson equation via rational approximation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work represents a significant advance in quantum algorithms for solving fractional differential equations, which model anomalous diffusion processes crucial in fields ranging from finance to materials science. The authors cleverly sidestep the inherent challenges of fractional operators by using rational approximation to transform the problem into a collection of standard partial differential equations that quantum computers can handle efficiently. Most impressively, their algorithm achieves dimension-independent scaling with respect to mesh size, potentially offering exponential speedups over classical methods for high-dimensional problems where traditional approaches suffer from the curse of dimensionality. While still theoretical, this breakthrough opens new possibilities for quantum advantage in computational physics and could eventually impact practical simulations in drug discovery, climate modeling, and financial risk analysis.

This paper presents a quantum algorithm for solving the fractional Poisson equation \((-Δ)^s u = f\) with \(s \in (0,1)\) on bounded domains. The proposed approach combines rational approximation techniques with quantum linear system solvers to achieve exponential quantum advantage. The rational approximation represents the inverse fractional Laplacian as a weighted sum of standard resolvents, transforming the original nonlocal problem into a collection of shifted integer-order partial differential equations. These equations are consolidated into a single large linear system through a modified right-hand side construction that simplifies the quantum implementation. To enable practical implementation, we develop explicit quantum circuits via the Schrödingerization technique, which converts the non-unitary dynamics of the linear system into a higher-dimensional Schrödinger-type equation, allowing the use of standard Hamiltonian simulation. The circuit construction leverages the decomposition of shift operators to realize the discrete Laplacian and employs controlled operations to implement the select oracle. Under finite difference discretization, we provide detailed algorithmic procedures utilizing block-encoding techniques for the coefficient matrices. A comprehensive complexity analysis demonstrates that the quantum algorithm achieves a dependence on the inverse mesh size \(h^{-1}\) that is independent of the spatial dimension \(d\), in stark contrast to classical methods which suffer from exponential growth in high dimensions. This establishes an exponential quantum advantage for high-dimensional fractional problems, effectively overcoming the curse of dimensionality that limits classical approaches.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-Safe Code Auditing: LLM-Assisted Static Analysis and Quantum-Aware Risk Scoring for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing practical challenges in quantum computing's transition from laboratory curiosity to real-world threat: how organizations can systematically identify and prioritize which cryptographic implementations in their massive codebases need urgent replacement before quantum computers break them. The team's clever approach combines traditional code analysis with large language models and even uses a quantum algorithm (VQE) to score risk levels, creating what appears to be the first comprehensive automated tool for post-quantum cryptography migration planning. With NIST's quantum-resistant standards now finalized, this kind of practical tooling could prove invaluable for the enormous task of securing software infrastructure before cryptographically relevant quantum computers arrive. The impressive recall rate of 100% suggests organizations could trust this system not to miss critical vulnerabilities, though the 72% precision indicates some manual verification would still be needed.

The impending arrival of cryptographically relevant quantum computers (CRQCs) threatens the security foundations of modern software: Shor's algorithm breaks RSA, ECDSA, ECDH, and Diffie-Hellman, while Grover's algorithm reduces the effective security of symmetric and hash-based schemes. Despite NIST standardising post-quantum cryptography (PQC) in 2024 (FIPS 203 ML-KEM, FIPS 204 ML-DSA, FIPS 205 SLH-DSA), most codebases lack automated tooling to inventory classical cryptographic usage and prioritise migration based on quantum risk. We present Quantum-Safe Code Auditor, a quantum-aware static analysis framework that combines (i) regex-based detection of 15 classes of quantum-vulnerable primitives, (ii) LLM-assisted contextual enrichment to classify usage and severity, and (iii) risk scoring via a Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) model implemented in Qiskit 2.x, incorporating qubit-cost estimates to prioritise findings. We evaluate the system across five open-source libraries -- python-rsa, python-ecdsa, python-jose, node-jsonwebtoken, and Bouncy Castle Java -- covering 5,775 findings. On a stratified sample of 602 labelled instances, we achieve 71.98% precision, 100% recall, and an F1 score of 83.71%. All code, data, and reproduction scripts are released as open-source.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between EPB and the Southeastern Quantum Collaborative represents an important step toward building the telecommunications infrastructure that quantum networks will eventually require. EPB, known for its advanced fiber optic network in Chattanooga, brings critical connectivity expertise to a collaborative that already includes major quantum companies like IBM, D-Wave, and IonQ. The move signals growing recognition that quantum computing's future depends not just on advancing the technology itself, but on developing the regional partnerships and infrastructure needed to support quantum applications at scale. As quantum technologies mature, collaboratives like SQC could become essential hubs for integrating quantum capabilities into existing business and research ecosystems across different regions.

EPB has joined the Southeastern Quantum Collaborative (SQC) as an inaugural member. Led by the University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), the SQC is a regional association of academic, industrial, and government organizations—including IBM, D-Wave, and IonQ—tasked with coordinating quantum information science research and workforce development across the Southeast. EPB’s role in the collaborative involves [...] The post EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Q-Day Just Got Closer: Three Papers in Three Months Are Rewriting the Quantum Threat Timeline
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a concerning acceleration in quantum cryptography research that's dramatically shortening the timeline for when quantum computers could break current encryption standards. The fact that three separate papers in just twelve months have each reduced the estimated quantum resources needed by an order of magnitude suggests we may be witnessing a cascade of algorithmic improvements that could make "Q-Day" arrive sooner than the widely cited 2030-2040 estimates. This development underscores the urgency for organizations to begin their migration to post-quantum cryptography now, rather than waiting for more definitive timelines. The convergence of multiple research breakthroughs in such a short timeframe indicates this isn't just theoretical progress, but potentially actionable intelligence that could inform nation-state quantum programs.

The quantum resources needed to break modern encryption have dropped by an order of magnitude since May 2025. Here is what happened, what it means, and what organizations should do about it. In fewer than twelve months, three research papers have sharply reduced the estimated quantum resources required to break the cryptographic systems that protect […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: STV Group and Post-Quantum Test Quantum-Secure Drone Communications
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between STV Group and Post-Quantum represents a crucial step toward securing military drone operations against future quantum computer attacks that could break current encryption methods. The successful testing of quantum-safe cryptography in active military environments demonstrates that post-quantum security isn't just theoretical anymore - it's being deployed in real-world, high-stakes scenarios where communication security can determine mission success or failure. This development is particularly significant given the widespread use of autonomous systems in current conflicts, highlighting how the quantum threat is driving immediate adoption of quantum-resistant technologies rather than waiting for large-scale quantum computers to emerge. The military's early adoption of post-quantum cryptography could accelerate its implementation across civilian infrastructure and commercial drone operations.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — STV Group, an European defence technology integrator with platforms deployed in Ukraine and allied theatres, and Post-Quantum, the UK cybersecurity company today announced successful testing of the world’s first quantum-safe drones for active deployment across allied theatres. As allied nations adapt to war-fighting defined by autonomous systems and drones, the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Launches SPOQC Satellite for Quantum Communications Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: This satellite launch represents a crucial step toward building truly global quantum communication networks, as space-based quantum key distribution could eventually enable unhackable communications between any two points on Earth. The SPOQC mission builds on years of ground-based quantum communication research and moves the technology into the challenging space environment, where quantum signals must survive the harsh conditions of orbital deployment. While current quantum communication is limited by the distance light can travel through fiber optic cables before quantum states degrade, satellites could potentially relay quantum-encrypted messages across continents without the security vulnerabilities of traditional methods. The UK's investment in this space-based quantum infrastructure positions the nation as a leader in the emerging quantum internet, though practical applications will likely require several more years of development and testing.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — A landmark space mission built on UK research excellence is set to accelerate progress in quantum communications, as the Heriot-Watt University led Integrated Quantum Networks (IQN) Hub launches the pioneering “Satellite Platform for Optical Quantum Communications” (SPOQC). Emerging from research and development efforts led by the preceding Quantum Communications Hub […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuSecure Joins NIST NCCoE Consortium for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration highlights the accelerating urgency around post-quantum cryptography implementation as organizations grapple with the practical realities of migrating from vulnerable legacy systems. QuSecure's participation in NIST's consortium represents a crucial bridge between standardized quantum-resistant algorithms and real-world deployment challenges that enterprises face today. The project addresses a critical gap in the quantum security landscape where having NIST-approved algorithms is just the first step - the harder challenge lies in seamlessly integrating these new cryptographic standards into existing infrastructure without disrupting operations. This initiative could significantly speed up post-quantum cryptography adoption across industries by providing concrete migration pathways and best practices.

QuSecure has announced its participation in the Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography Project consortium, organized by the National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence (NCCoE). This collaboration is designed to assist organizations in transitioning from current public-key algorithms to NIST-standardized quantum-resistant alternatives. The project addresses the technical and operational challenges associated with replacing legacy cryptography that is vulnerable [...] The post QuSecure Joins NIST NCCoE Consortium for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Research Team Finds Useful Quantum Computers Could Be Built with as Few as 10,000 Qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a potentially game-changing shift in quantum computing scalability, as previous estimates suggested that fault-tolerant quantum computers would need millions of physical qubits to perform useful calculations. The collaboration between Caltech theorists and Oratomic demonstrates how academic-industry partnerships are driving practical breakthroughs in error correction, one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges. If validated, this 10,000-qubit threshold could dramatically accelerate the timeline for commercially viable quantum computers, bringing applications like drug discovery and cryptography breaking from decades away to potentially within the next 10-15 years. The work highlights how clever algorithmic and hardware innovations can sometimes overcome what seemed like insurmountable physical limitations in quantum systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today’s rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuSecure Collaborating with NIST’s National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence to Address Post-Quantum Algorithm Migration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between QuSecure and NIST's National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence represents a crucial step in preparing for the post-quantum cryptography transition that organizations worldwide must navigate as quantum computers threaten current encryption methods. The partnership highlights how the cybersecurity industry is moving beyond theoretical discussions about quantum threats to practical implementation challenges, focusing on developing real-world migration strategies that organizations can actually execute. This work is particularly timely given that NIST finalized its post-quantum cryptographic standards in 2024, and many organizations are now grappling with the complex task of upgrading their security infrastructure before quantum computers become capable enough to break existing encryption. The emphasis on "cryptographic agility" suggests they're working on solutions that will make future algorithm transitions smoother, learning from the current migration challenges to build more adaptable security systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuSecure™, Inc., the market leader in post-quantum cybersecurity and cryptographic agility, today announced it is collaborating with the National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence (NCCoE) in the Migration to Post-Quantum Cryptography Project Consortium to bring awareness to the issues involved in migrating to post-quantum algorithms and to develop practices to ease migration from current public-key […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: London Tech Week 2026 to Showcase Global Deep Tech Innovators in Space, Robotics, Sciences, Quantum and AI
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals London's ambitious push to position itself as a global quantum computing hub, leveraging the UK's strong academic foundations in quantum research from institutions like Oxford and Cambridge. The timing is particularly strategic as the quantum industry moves from laboratory demonstrations toward practical applications, with governments worldwide recognizing quantum technologies as critical for national competitiveness. London Tech Week's focus on bridging the gap between scientific breakthroughs and economic advantage reflects the urgent need to translate quantum research into commercial viability before the current quantum winter potentially stalls progress. The event could serve as a crucial platform for fostering international collaboration and investment in European quantum initiatives at a pivotal moment for the field.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — London Tech Week 2026 (8-12 June 2026) has announced its dedicated Deep Tech Stage, exploring the most exciting breakthroughs in space, robotics, materials, life sciences, AI and quantum computing. At a time when governments and industry are racing to turn scientific breakthroughs into economic and strategic advantage, the programme is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Podcast with Tom Darras, CEO and Co-founder, Welinq
item.description:
Curator's Take: This podcast offers valuable insights into quantum networking, a critical but often overlooked piece of the quantum computing puzzle that could solve the scaling challenges facing current quantum processors. Welinq's focus on quantum memories and entanglement distribution represents a key enabling technology for creating distributed quantum computing clusters, potentially allowing smaller quantum processors to work together as larger, more powerful systems. The discussion of quantum-safe connectivity between data centers is particularly timely as organizations begin preparing for the post-quantum cryptography transition. Tom Darras provides a clear technical breakdown of the building blocks needed to make quantum networks practical, making this essential listening for understanding how quantum computing might scale beyond individual machines.

Yuval Boger interviews Tom Darras, CEO and co-founder of Welinq. They discuss how quantum networking uses shared entanglement to interconnect quantum processors, enabling modular scale-out clusters and quantum-safe connectivity between data centers. Tom explains the technical building blocks—qubit-photon interfaces, optical networks, entangled photon sources, and especially quantum memories—as well as the performance metrics that matter [...] The post Podcast with Tom Darras, CEO and Co-founder, Welinq appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UMass Amherst and UCSB Demonstrate Photonic Chip Technology for Miniaturized Quantum Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a crucial step toward making quantum computers practically deployable by dramatically shrinking the footprint of trapped-ion quantum systems. The collaboration between UMass Amherst and UCSB tackles one of the biggest obstacles facing trapped-ion quantum computers: the need for room-sized laser and optical equipment that has made these systems impractical for most real-world applications. By integrating these complex optical components onto photonic chips, the researchers are paving the way for quantum computers that could actually fit in laboratories and data centers rather than requiring specialized facilities. This miniaturization approach could give trapped-ion systems a significant advantage in the race to build commercially viable quantum computers, as it addresses scalability and deployment challenges that have long plagued this promising quantum computing platform.

Researchers from UMass Amherst and the University of California Santa Barbara (UCSB) have demonstrated an integrated "system-on-a-chip" technology that replaces room-sized laser and optical components with miniaturized photonic chips. Led by Assistant Professor Robert Niffenegger and Professor Daniel Blumenthal, the team utilized trapped-ion technology to perform qubit and clock operations on a chip-scale device. This [...] The post UMass Amherst and UCSB Demonstrate Photonic Chip Technology for Miniaturized Quantum Systems appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone marks a crucial step toward scalable quantum computing by demonstrating that silicon - the backbone of classical computing - can successfully perform logical quantum operations with built-in error correction. The achievement is particularly significant because it combines silicon's natural advantages of long qubit coherence times and compatibility with existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure, potentially accelerating the path to practical quantum computers. By proving that logical operations can work in silicon-based systems, this research bridges the gap between today's experimental quantum devices and tomorrow's fault-tolerant quantum processors that could be manufactured using proven chip fabrication techniques. This development strengthens silicon's position as a leading candidate for quantum computing platforms that could eventually be produced at scale using modified versions of existing semiconductor fabs.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial milestone where quantum computers have moved beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations to actually reproducing real experimental data from national laboratories, marking a significant step toward practical quantum advantage in materials science. The ability to accurately simulate magnetic materials represents a major validation of quantum computing's potential, as these systems involve complex quantum many-body interactions that are notoriously difficult for classical computers to handle efficiently. This achievement suggests we're approaching the point where quantum simulators could accelerate the discovery of new materials with exotic properties, potentially revolutionizing fields from energy storage to superconductivity. The fact that quantum results are now matching established experimental benchmarks gives researchers confidence that these systems are ready for more ambitious materials discovery challenges.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: creating and maintaining entanglement at the scale needed for practical quantum advantage. While the summary presents familiar promises of quantum computing, the focus on "scalable entanglement" points to a critical bottleneck that researchers are actively working to solve - current quantum systems can only maintain entangled states among relatively small numbers of qubits before decoherence destroys the quantum properties that make these computers powerful. The emphasis on scalability suggests this work addresses the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and the fault-tolerant quantum computers needed for real-world applications. Successfully unlocking scalable entanglement would represent a major step toward quantum systems that can tackle problems beyond the reach of classical supercomputers.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dual-rail superconducting qubits generate high-fidelity logical entanglement, study finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial step toward building more robust quantum computers by demonstrating that dual-rail superconducting qubits can create high-fidelity logical entanglement between error-corrected quantum bits. Unlike physical qubits that are prone to noise and errors, logical qubits encode quantum information across multiple physical qubits to enable error correction, making this achievement essential for practical quantum computing applications. The ability to generate reliable entanglement between logical qubits brings us closer to fault-tolerant quantum computers that could maintain quantum coherence long enough to solve real-world problems. This work builds on recent advances in quantum error correction and could accelerate the timeline for quantum computers to achieve genuine advantages over classical systems in commercially relevant tasks.

Quantum computers, systems that process information leveraging quantum mechanical effects, could outperform classical computers on some advanced tasks. These systems rely on qubits, the fundamental units of quantum information, that become linked via an effect known as quantum entanglement and share a unified quantum state.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws. Unlike typical quantum systems where entanglement grows with the boundary area between regions, Motzkin chains exhibit logarithmic scaling that makes them notoriously difficult to simulate on classical computers, yet they hold deep connections to topological phases and even string theory through AdS/CFT correspondence. The researchers' proposed Rydberg atom platform offers a realistic experimental route to create these mathematical constructs in the lab, potentially opening doors to studying a whole new class of highly entangled quantum phases that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work exemplifies how programmable quantum simulators are becoming powerful tools for exploring exotic quantum matter that exists at the edge of what's computationally tractable.

Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: NRFC Investment Accelerates SQC’s Atomic-Scale Manufacturing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Silicon Quantum Computing's unique atomic-scale manufacturing approach, which positions them as the sole company worldwide capable of fabricating quantum chips with atomic precision. The AUD$20 million from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation not only validates SQC's silicon-based quantum technology but also strengthens Australia's position in the global quantum race, particularly in the critical hardware manufacturing sector where precision at the atomic level remains one of the field's greatest technical challenges. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's claim of already delivering quantum-enhanced AI products to enterprise and government clients today, suggesting their technology has moved beyond pure research into practical applications. This combination of manufacturing precision and commercial readiness could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computing deployment, especially in applications where silicon-based quantum systems offer advantages over other qubit technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) has invested AUD$20 million in Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC), a global leader in the field of quantum computing. SQC is the only company in the world capable of manufacturing quantum chips with atomic precision while delivering quantum enhanced-AI products for enterprises and governments today. It is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Global control via quantum actuators
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces an elegant solution to one of quantum computing's biggest architectural challenges: how to perform selective quantum operations without needing individual control over every qubit.
